paper_title: Oxygen Isotope Age Trends in Tree Rings

methods.description: Process for obtaining tree and timber samples, selecting them based on suitability for oxygen isotope chronology construction, trimming ring sequences, and separating modern and preindustrial samples.

steps.description: Two sets of samples are used in this study: 17 living or recently felled trees and 32 oak timbers, from historic buildings of the tenth to nineteenth centuries, obtained from the Oxford Dendrochronology Laboratory archive.
steps.searchable_summary: Obtain tree and timber samples from specified sources.

steps.description: The samples are representative of the material available for constructing a long oxygen isotope chronology for the UK. Very few long ring sequences are available or suitable for isotope chronology construction, because the decline in ring widths with age makes it increasingly difficult to separate the latewood, so all series are trimmed to a maximum of 100 years.
steps.searchable_summary: Select samples suitable for isotope chronology and trim ring sequences to a maximum length.
outputs: selected and trimmed tree ring series

steps.description: Modern and preindustrial trees are considered separately because of the potential for anthropogenic trends in climate, atmospheric chemistry, air pollution, and forest management to influence the results of the modern trees.
steps.searchable_summary: Separate samples into modern and preindustrial groups.
inputs: selected and trimmed tree ring series

methods.description: Laboratory procedure to extract alpha-cellulose from latewood of tree rings and analyze its stable oxygen isotope composition using mass spectrometry, reporting results in delta notation.

steps.description: The latewood of each annual ring was cut into thin slivers (approximately 40 μm thick) under magnification
steps.searchable_summary: Cut latewood from annual rings into thin slivers.

steps.description: and the alpha-cellulose extracted (Loader et al., 1997),
steps.searchable_summary: Extract alpha-cellulose from latewood slivers.

steps.description: homogenized,
steps.searchable_summary: Homogenize the extracted alpha-cellulose.

steps.description: and freeze-dried.
steps.searchable_summary: Freeze-dry the homogenized alpha-cellulose.

steps.description: Between 0.30 and 0.35 mg was weighed into silver capsules
steps.searchable_summary: Weigh a specific amount of freeze-dried cellulose into silver capsules.

steps.description: and pyrolyzed for stable oxygen isotope analysis by mass spectrometry.
steps.searchable_summary: Pyrolyze the cellulose samples.

steps.description: Results are expressed as per mil (‰) relative to Vienna Standard Mean Ocean Water using the delta notation (δ 18 O; Coplen, 1995).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform stable oxygen isotope analysis using mass spectrometry and express results in delta notation (δ18O) relative to VSMOW.

methods.description: Statistical procedures used to investigate linear and non-linear age-related trends in the stable oxygen isotope data from individual tree ring series and averaged series.

steps.description: We investigate possible linear age trends in the two groups, first using the conventional "slope of the mean" method of fitting a simple linear regression line through the mean of the age-aligned (by ring number) series.
steps.searchable_summary: Investigate linear age trends using the 'slope of the mean' method.
inputs: δ18O values for each tree ring series
outputs: mean δ18O series aligned by age

steps.description: fitting a simple linear regression line through the mean of the age-aligned (by ring number) series.
steps.searchable_summary: Fit a simple linear regression line to the mean age-aligned δ18O series.
inputs: mean δ18O series aligned by age
outputs: linear regression model (slope of the mean)

steps.description: a more direct approach is to examine those trends in the individual trees, and then to compile them in a way that allows them to be compared and averaged. We achieved this by fitting simple linear regression lines to the individual series (rather than the mean series) and plotting the regression lines for all trees aligned at a common point of origin ("mean of the slope" method).
steps.searchable_summary: Investigate age trends in individual series using the 'mean of the slope' method.
inputs: δ18O values for each individual tree ring series

steps.description: fitting simple linear regression lines to the individual series (rather than the mean series)
steps.searchable_summary: Fit simple linear regression lines to each individual tree ring δ18O series.
inputs: δ18O values for each individual tree ring series
outputs: linear regression model for each series

steps.description: plotting the regression lines for all trees aligned at a common point of origin ("mean of the slope" method).
steps.searchable_summary: Plot individual regression lines aligned at a common origin.
inputs: linear regression model for each series

steps.description: For the mean of the slope method statistical significance is assessed by using the binomial test to determine the probability of the split of positive and negative slopes occurring by chance
steps.searchable_summary: Assess statistical significance of the mean of the slope using a binomial test on the distribution of positive/negative slopes.

steps.description: and by calculating the 95% confidence limits around the mean of the slope values.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate the 95% confidence limits for the mean of the slope values.

steps.description: We also use second-order polynomials to test whether nonlinear regression lines would provide a much better fit to potential age trends.
steps.searchable_summary: Fit second-order polynomial regression models to test for non-linear age trends.
inputs: δ18O values for each tree ring series

steps.description: All probabilities are two-tailed.
steps.searchable_summary: Ensure all statistical tests are two-tailed.

methods.description: Comparing the identified age trends in the isotope data from modern and preindustrial trees with external climate data (precipitation) and a stable oxygen isotope master chronology.

steps.description: Trends in the isotope data from the modern trees are compared with those in the England and Wales precipitation record for May to August (Alexander & Jones, 2001;Young et al., 2015).
steps.searchable_summary: Obtain England and Wales precipitation record for May to August.
outputs: England and Wales May-August precipitation data

steps.description: Trends in the isotope data from the modern trees are compared with those in the England and Wales precipitation record for May to August
steps.searchable_summary: Compare age trends in modern tree δ18O data with trends in May-August precipitation data.
inputs: age trends in modern tree δ18O
England and Wales May-August precipitation data

steps.description: Trends in the preindustrial series are compared with relevant sections of a long stable oxygen isotope "master chronology" based on pooled latewood cellulose from 10 separate trees (Loader et al., 2019).
steps.searchable_summary: Obtain relevant sections of the stable oxygen isotope master chronology.
outputs: stable oxygen isotope master chronology data

steps.description: Trends in the preindustrial series are compared with relevant sections of a long stable oxygen isotope "master chronology"
steps.searchable_summary: Compare age trends in preindustrial tree δ18O data with trends in the master chronology.
inputs: age trends in preindustrial tree δ18O
stable oxygen isotope master chronology data

methods.description: Generating synthetic time series data with no inherent age trend to test whether the age trend analysis methods (slope of the mean, mean of the slope) produce spurious trends.

steps.description: To test whether the contrasting approaches can produce spurious age trends, when there are no real age trends in the constituent series, we generated time series of random numbers.
steps.searchable_summary: Generate time series of random numbers.

steps.description: These series have a similar range of mean values to the preindustrial timbers used in this study
steps.searchable_summary: Scale or adjust random series to have a similar range of mean values as the real data.
inputs: mean values of preindustrial timber δ18O

steps.description: and we varied the length of the series to exactly reflect the change in replication,
steps.searchable_summary: Vary the length of the random series to match the replication changes in the real data.
outputs: random number time series with varying lengths

steps.description: a procedure that was repeated 100 times.
steps.searchable_summary: Repeat the data generation and analysis procedure 100 times.
outputs: 100 sets of simulated data and analysis results

steps.description: The conventional slope of the mean approach was then applied by fitting a linear regression
steps.searchable_summary: Apply the 'slope of the mean' analysis method to the simulated random data.
outputs: mean simulated series aligned by age

steps.description: fitting a linear regression
steps.searchable_summary: Fit a linear regression to the mean simulated series.
inputs: mean simulated series aligned by age
outputs: linear regression model (simulated slope of the mean)
slope value (simulated slope of the mean)

steps.description: and the mean of the slope value was also calculated.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate the 'mean of the slope' value for the simulated random data.
outputs: individual slope values for simulated series
